title: aciphylla traillii var. cartilaginea
intro: Aciphylla traillii var. cartilaginea, commonly known as New Zealand carrot or parsnip tree, is a plant species native to New Zealand. It has been used in traditional Maori medicine for its health benefits, which include treating fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The therapeutic actions of this plant are attributed to its anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antimicrobial properties, making it useful for wound healing and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Aciphylla traillii var. cartilaginea include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties.

preparations: Aciphylla traillii var. cartilaginea has many herbal preparations, such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, that are used for medicinal purposes. side_effects: Aciphylla traillii var. cartilaginea can have side effects if used improperly, such as gastrointestinal issues, including diarrhea, nausea, and stomach cramps. In severe cases, consuming large amounts of the plant can lead to kidney damage and electrolyte imbalances, causing symptoms like muscle weakness and fatigue.
